enrichment.short_description: Clearco is a Toronto fintech that provides revenue-based financing to ecommerce brands, offering fast, non-dilutive capital for inventory, marketing, and growth.
enrichment.seo_title: Clearco Review: Revenue-Based Financing for Ecommerce
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Clearco offers non-dilutive, revenue-based financing for ecommerce brands. See how funding works, fees, integrations, pros, cons, and who it's built for.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Clearco (formerly Clearbanc) is a Toronto, Canada-based financial technology company founded in 2015 by Andrew D'Souza and Michele Romanow. It provides revenue-based financing (RBF) to ecommerce and consumer brands as an alternative to venture capital, bank loans, or credit cards. Rather than taking equity or requiring personal guarantees, Clearco advances capital against a business's future revenue and collects repayment as a percentage of daily or weekly sales until the advance plus a flat fee is repaid. The company has deployed more than $3 billion in funding to over 10,000 businesses since inception. Clearco's core products include rolling funding capacity (an ongoing, renewable line of capital), fixed funding capacity (a lump-sum advance), cash advances, and invoice funding for B2B ecommerce sellers waiting on receivables. To underwrite a business, Clearco connects directly to a company's sales and payment data through integrations with Shopify, Amazon, BigCommerce, Square, Stripe, and PayPal, using that transaction history to make funding decisions in as little as 24 hours rather than the weeks typical of bank underwriting. There are no board seats, no blanket liens on all business assets, and businesses can repay early without penalty, which can reduce the total cost of capital. Clearco is best suited for direct-to-consumer and marketplace sellers that have consistent, trackable online revenue and need capital for inventory purchases, paid advertising spend, or working capital to fund growth without diluting ownership. It is not a traditional term-loan or line-of-credit product in the banking sense, and it does not publish a fixed subscription price list the way SaaS tools do; instead, the cost of capital (expressed as a flat fee) is calculated per funding offer based on the size of the advance, the business's revenue history, and repayment terms. Because eligibility depends on connecting real sales and banking data, Clearco is generally not a fit for pre-revenue startups, service businesses without recurring transaction data, or companies outside its supported ecommerce platforms. Competitors and alternatives in the revenue-based financing and ecommerce-lending space include Wayflyer, Shopify Capital, Uncapped, and traditional SBA or bank lines of credit. Clearco has raised roughly $775 million in venture funding from investors including SoftBank and Inovia Capital, and it reports that a majority of funded businesses return for repeat funding rounds, an indicator that many customers use Clearco as a recurring working-capital tool rather than a one-time loan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Clearco was founded in 2015 in Toronto by Andrew D'Souza and Michele Romanow as Clearbanc, rebranding to Clearco in 2021. The company built its business around a simple premise: ecommerce founders were routinely giving up equity to venture investors just to fund inventory and ad spend, expenses that are predictable and directly tied to revenue. Clearco replaced that model with revenue-based financing, advancing capital in exchange for a fixed fee and a share of future revenue rather than an ownership stake.

Since launch, Clearco has funded more than 10,000 ecommerce and consumer brands and deployed over $3 billion in capital. It underwrites deals by connecting directly to a company's sales, ad spend, and payment platforms, which lets it evaluate and approve funding requests far faster than a conventional bank loan process.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Clearco offers several funding structures: rolling funding capacity that renews as it's repaid, fixed funding capacity for one-time capital needs, cash advances, and invoice funding for businesses waiting on B2B receivables. Funding decisions are typically made within 24 hours once a business connects its sales and payment accounts.

The platform integrates with the ecommerce and payments tools most DTC brands already use, including Shopify, Amazon, BigCommerce, Square, Stripe, and PayPal, pulling transaction history automatically instead of requiring manual financial statements. Repayment scales with revenue, businesses pay more when sales are strong and less during slower periods, and early repayment can reduce the total fee owed.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Clearco does not use a subscription pricing model. Instead, it charges a flat fee on the capital advanced, calculated per offer based on the funding amount, the business's revenue history, and the agreed repayment terms, so there is no single published "starting price." Businesses see their specific fee and repayment terms only after connecting their sales data and receiving a funding offer.

Because pricing is individualized, prospective customers typically need to apply and link their store or payment accounts to get an exact quote. There are no setup fees, no equity given up, and no personal guarantees required, but the total cost of capital varies by deal.

features.title: Rolling funding capacity
features.description: An ongoing, renewable capital line that businesses can draw against repeatedly as they repay, rather than reapplying for each funding need.

features.title: Fixed funding capacity
features.description: A one-time lump-sum advance sized for a specific inventory purchase, marketing push, or growth initiative.

features.title: Invoice funding
features.description: Advances against outstanding B2B invoices so ecommerce sellers with wholesale or marketplace receivables aren't stuck waiting on payment terms.

features.title: Ecommerce and payments integrations
features.description: Direct connections to Shopify, Amazon, BigCommerce, Square, Stripe, and PayPal let Clearco underwrite based on real transaction history.

features.title: Fast funding decisions
features.description: Funding offers can be issued in as little as 24 hours after a business connects its data, far faster than typical bank underwriting.

features.title: No equity or personal guarantees
features.description: Capital is provided without taking board seats, ownership stakes, or requiring personal guarantees from founders.

features.title: Revenue-based repayment
features.description: Repayment is collected as a percentage of ongoing revenue, so payments flex up or down with the business's actual sales.

features.title: Early repayment savings
features.description: Businesses that repay advances ahead of schedule can reduce the total fee owed on the capital.

pros: No equity dilution or board seats required to access capital
Funding decisions can be made in as little as 24 hours
Repayment scales with revenue rather than a fixed monthly payment
Deep integrations with the ecommerce platforms brands already use
Track record of over $3 billion deployed to 10,000+ businesses
cons: Not a fit for pre-revenue startups or businesses without trackable online sales history
Requires connecting sensitive sales and banking data for underwriting
Total cost of capital (flat fee) isn't published and varies per deal
Limited mainly to ecommerce and consumer brands rather than all business types

faqs.question: What is Clearco and how is it different from a bank loan?
faqs.answer: Clearco is a revenue-based financing provider for ecommerce brands. Instead of a fixed-rate loan or line of credit underwritten on credit history, Clearco advances capital based on a business's actual sales data and collects repayment as a percentage of revenue until the advance plus a flat fee is repaid.

faqs.question: Does Clearco take equity in exchange for funding?
faqs.answer: No. Clearco's financing is non-dilutive: it does not take equity, board seats, or require personal guarantees from founders.

faqs.question: How fast can a business get funded through Clearco?
faqs.answer: Once a business connects its sales, payments, and ad accounts, Clearco can issue a funding decision in as little as 24 hours, though the exact timeline depends on the completeness of the data provided.

faqs.question: What platforms does Clearco integrate with?
faqs.answer: Clearco connects with major ecommerce and payments platforms including Shopify, Amazon, BigCommerce, Square, Stripe, and PayPal to evaluate revenue and underwrite funding offers.

faqs.question: How much does Clearco funding cost?
faqs.answer: Clearco charges a flat fee on the capital advanced rather than a traditional interest rate. The exact fee depends on the funding amount, revenue history, and repayment terms, and is only shown after a business applies and connects its data.

faqs.question: Who is Clearco best suited for?
faqs.answer: Clearco is designed for ecommerce and consumer brands with consistent, trackable online revenue that need capital for inventory, advertising, or working capital without giving up equity.

use_cases.title: Inventory financing for DTC brands
use_cases.description: Direct-to-consumer brands use Clearco advances to buy inventory ahead of demand spikes or seasonal peaks without tying up cash flow.

use_cases.title: Performance marketing spend
use_cases.description: Brands fund paid advertising campaigns on channels like Meta and Google using Clearco capital, then repay as the campaigns drive incremental revenue.

use_cases.title: Working capital for B2B ecommerce sellers
use_cases.description: Wholesale and marketplace sellers use invoice funding to bridge the gap between fulfilling orders and receiving payment on outstanding invoices.

enrichment.short_description: ClearML is an open-source MLOps and AI infrastructure platform for experiment tracking, data versioning, orchestration, and model deployment, self-hosted or managed.
enrichment.seo_title: ClearML Review: Open-Source MLOps Platform & Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ClearML is an open-source MLOps platform for experiment tracking, data versioning, pipelines, and model deployment. Compare features, pricing, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ClearML is an open-source MLOps and AI infrastructure platform founded in 2016 and headquartered at 114 Yigal Alon Street in Tel Aviv, Israel. It consolidates the tools machine learning teams typically stitch together separately, experiment tracking, dataset and model versioning, pipeline orchestration, hyperparameter optimization, compute-resource scheduling, and model serving, into a single open-source codebase available on GitHub under the clearml organization. The self-hosted, open-source edition is free and includes production-grade capabilities such as automatic experiment logging, artifact and model repositories, pipeline definition and orchestration, and an agent that can queue and run jobs across available compute. ClearML also offers a hosted SaaS version and enterprise deployment options (VPC, on-premises, hybrid, and air-gapped) for teams that want a managed experience or additional governance controls. Commercial pricing is tiered: a free Community plan supports up to three users with 100GB of artifact storage, 1GB of metric events, and 1 million API calls per month; a Pro plan costs $15 per user per month for up to ten users, adding cloud auto-scaling across AWS, GCP, and Azure, hyperparameter optimization, pipeline triggers, and dashboards, with additional storage, metrics, API calls, and compute billed on a pay-as-you-go basis; and Scale and Enterprise plans use custom quoted pricing for organizations running larger GPU fleets (roughly 8 to 48+ GPUs), adding features like fine-tuning workflows, vector database integration, Kubernetes and Slurm/PBS integration, SSO, role-based access control, and white-glove support. ClearML is used primarily by machine learning engineers, data scientists, and MLOps teams that need to track experiments reproducibly, manage datasets and model versions, and orchestrate training and inference pipelines across cloud or on-premises GPU infrastructure. Because it is open source and self-hostable, it appeals particularly to organizations with data-residency, security, or air-gapped requirements that rule out fully SaaS-only MLOps tools. Its closest competitors include Weights & Biases, MLflow, Neptune.ai, and Comet, with ClearML differentiating itself primarily through the breadth of its open-source feature set (covering orchestration and compute scheduling in addition to experiment tracking) and its flexible self-hosted-to-enterprise deployment path.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ClearML was founded in 2016 in Tel Aviv, Israel, and built its reputation as one of the more complete open-source MLOps toolkits available, covering experiment tracking, data versioning, pipeline orchestration, and compute-resource management in one project rather than requiring teams to combine several separate tools. The core codebase is open source on GitHub, and the company also sells hosted and enterprise versions for teams that want a managed platform or additional controls.

ClearML is aimed at machine learning engineers and MLOps teams running training and inference workloads, whether on a handful of GPUs or across large multi-cloud clusters. Its open-source, self-hostable nature makes it a common choice for teams with strict data-residency or air-gapped infrastructure requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform automatically logs experiments, hyperparameters, metrics, and artifacts with minimal code changes, and maintains a versioned model and dataset repository so results can be reproduced later. Pipelines can be defined, orchestrated, and scheduled directly in ClearML, and an agent-based system queues and executes jobs across whatever compute is available, including auto-scaling across AWS, GCP, and Azure on paid tiers.

Higher tiers add hyperparameter optimization, pipeline triggers, and dashboards for team-wide visibility, while enterprise deployments add Kubernetes and Slurm/PBS integration, fractional GPU allocation, vector database integration for LLM workflows, SSO, and role-based access control for larger or regulated organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ClearML's Community plan is free for up to three users and includes core experiment tracking, data management, and pipeline features with 100GB of artifact storage and 1 million API calls per month. The Pro plan costs $15 per user per month for up to ten users and adds cloud auto-scaling and hyperparameter optimization, with extra storage, metrics, and API usage billed pay-as-you-go beyond the included allotment.

Scale and Enterprise plans are custom-quoted and intended for organizations running dedicated GPU infrastructure, whether in their own VPC, on-premises, or in a hybrid setup, with pricing based on compute scale and the specific enterprise features required rather than a flat per-user rate.

features.title: Experiment tracking
features.description: Automatic logging of hyperparameters, metrics, code, and artifacts for every training run, with minimal instrumentation required.

features.title: Data and model versioning
features.description: A built-in repository for datasets and model artifacts that tracks lineage across experiments.

features.title: Pipeline orchestration
features.description: Define, schedule, and run multi-step ML pipelines, with triggers available on paid tiers.

features.title: Compute orchestration and auto-scaling
features.description: An agent-based queueing system that runs jobs across available compute, with auto-scaling across AWS, GCP, and Azure on the Pro tier and above.

features.title: Hyperparameter optimization
features.description: Automated search over hyperparameters to find better-performing model configurations.

features.title: Model serving
features.description: Deploy trained models for inference as part of the same platform used for training and experiment tracking.

features.title: Self-hosted or managed deployment
features.description: Run the open-source edition on your own infrastructure, or use ClearML's hosted SaaS or enterprise VPC/on-prem/air-gapped options.

features.title: Enterprise access controls
features.description: SSO, role-based access control, and configuration vaults available on Enterprise plans for regulated environments.

pros: Open-source core is free and self-hostable with production-grade features
Covers experiment tracking, data versioning, and orchestration in one platform
Flexible deployment from self-hosted to fully managed enterprise VPC/on-prem
Pro tier is affordably priced per user relative to enterprise-only MLOps competitors
cons: Free Community tier caps out at 3 users, limiting it to small teams
Pay-as-you-go overage charges on storage, metrics, and API calls can add up for heavy usage
Enterprise and Scale pricing is not public and requires a custom quote
Feature depth across so many capabilities means a learning curve for teams new to MLOps

faqs.question: Is ClearML open source?
faqs.answer: Yes. ClearML's core platform is open source and available on GitHub, and can be self-hosted for free with production-grade experiment tracking, data versioning, pipeline, and orchestration features.

faqs.question: How much does ClearML cost?
faqs.answer: The self-hosted open-source edition is free. The hosted Pro plan costs $15 per user per month for up to 10 users, while Scale and Enterprise plans for larger GPU deployments use custom quoted pricing.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ClearML Community and Pro plans?
faqs.answer: Community is free for up to 3 users with core experiment tracking and pipeline features. Pro adds cloud auto-scaling across AWS, GCP, and Azure, hyperparameter optimization, pipeline triggers, and dashboards for up to 10 users.

faqs.question: Can ClearML be self-hosted or run on-premises?
faqs.answer: Yes. In addition to the free open-source self-hosted edition, ClearML offers Enterprise deployment options including VPC, on-premises, hybrid, and air-gapped installations.

faqs.question: Who uses ClearML?
faqs.answer: ClearML is used by machine learning engineers, data scientists, and MLOps teams to track experiments, version datasets and models, and orchestrate training and inference pipelines across cloud or on-premises compute.

faqs.question: What are ClearML's main alternatives?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Weights & Biases, MLflow, Neptune.ai, and Comet, though ClearML differentiates itself by also including pipeline and compute orchestration in its open-source offering.

use_cases.title: Experiment tracking for research teams
use_cases.description: ML researchers log and compare training runs automatically, making results reproducible across a team without manual spreadsheets.

use_cases.title: GPU cluster orchestration
use_cases.description: MLOps teams queue and auto-scale training jobs across cloud or on-prem GPU clusters using ClearML's agent-based orchestration.

use_cases.title: Regulated or air-gapped ML environments
use_cases.description: Organizations with strict data residency requirements self-host ClearML on-premises or in an air-gapped enterprise deployment.

features: Pay-as-you-go overage on storage, metrics, API calls, compute

enrichment.short_description: Clearscope is an AI-powered content optimization platform that helps writers and SEO teams create content that ranks in Google and gets cited in AI answer engines.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Clearscope is an AI content optimization tool for SEO and AI search visibility. See pricing plans, features, integrations, pros and cons before you buy.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Clearscope is a content optimization platform founded in 2016 by Bernard Huang and Kevin Su, originally in San Francisco and now headquartered in Austin, Texas. It helps content marketers, SEO teams, and writers create pages designed to rank well in Google and, increasingly, to be surfaced and cited by AI answer engines such as ChatGPT and Gemini. Rather than focusing on backlinks or technical site audits like many broader SEO suites, Clearscope centers on semantic content quality: it analyzes top-ranking pages for a target keyword and generates a content grade along with recommended terms, related topics, and readability guidance that writers use while drafting. The product integrates directly into Google Docs and WordPress so writers can see and act on optimization suggestions inside the tools they already use, and it has added AI-search-specific capabilities including prompt tracking across ChatGPT and Gemini and brand-visibility monitoring in AI-generated answers. Clearscope's pricing is a three-tier subscription: Essentials at $129 per month includes 50 tracked prompts, 50 pages, and 20 monthly topic explorations and drafts; Business at $399 per month raises those limits to 300 tracked prompts and 300 pages with a dedicated account manager; and Enterprise uses custom pricing with added features like SSO, crawler whitelisting, and custom agreements. All plans include unlimited sharing and no long-term contract requirement, with additional pages or drafts available as paid add-ons beyond plan limits. Clearscope is a small, bootstrapped, profitable company with a compact remote-first team, and reports more than 1,000 customers including large brands such as Adobe, Shopify, Intuit, HubSpot, Deloitte, and Condé Nast. It is best suited for content teams, SEO specialists, and agencies that produce a steady volume of organic-search content and need a structured, data-backed way to brief and grade writers, rather than for one-off bloggers or businesses without an ongoing content production process. Its closest competitors in the content-optimization category include Surfer SEO, MarketMuse, and Frase, with Clearscope generally positioned as a premium, quality-focused option relative to lower-priced alternatives.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Clearscope was founded in 2016 by Bernard Huang and Kevin Su to help content teams write pages that are more likely to rank for a target search query, using data pulled from the pages already ranking well rather than generic SEO checklists. The company has since expanded its focus beyond traditional Google rankings to also track how brands and content appear in AI-generated answers from tools like ChatGPT and Gemini.

The platform is built around a simple workflow: enter a target keyword, get a content report with a grade and recommended terms based on top-ranking competitors, then write or revise content directly in Google Docs or WordPress while Clearscope scores it in real time.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Clearscope generates a content grade and term recommendations by analyzing top-ranking pages for a given keyword, and surfaces related topics and questions writers should address to match search intent. Its Google Docs and WordPress integrations let writers see live optimization feedback without leaving their normal writing tools.

Newer capabilities extend beyond classic SEO into AI search visibility, including tracking how often a brand or page is referenced in ChatGPT and Gemini responses for tracked prompts, along with query analysis and AI-driven content recommendations aimed at improving that visibility.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Clearscope offers three subscription tiers billed monthly: Essentials at $129/month (50 tracked prompts, 50 pages, 20 topic explorations, 20 drafts), Business at $399/month (300 tracked prompts, 300 pages, a dedicated account manager), and Enterprise at custom pricing (adds SSO, crawler whitelisting, and custom agreements).

All tiers include prompt tracking across ChatGPT and Gemini, brand visibility monitoring, and unlimited sharing with no long-term contract required; additional pages and drafts beyond a plan's limits are available as paid add-ons.

features.title: Content grading and term recommendations
features.description: Analyzes top-ranking pages for a target keyword and generates a content grade plus recommended terms and related topics.

features.title: Google Docs integration
features.description: Live content scoring and term suggestions appear directly inside Google Docs while writers draft.

features.title: WordPress integration
features.description: Optimize and grade content directly within WordPress without exporting to a separate tool.

features.title: AI search prompt tracking
features.description: Tracks how a brand or page appears in ChatGPT and Gemini responses for a defined set of prompts.

features.title: Brand visibility monitoring
features.description: Monitors how often and how favorably a brand is mentioned in AI-generated answers.

features.title: Topic and query analysis
features.description: Surfaces related topics, questions, and subtopics competitors cover that a piece of content may be missing.

features.title: Unlimited sharing
features.description: Reports and content grades can be shared across a team or with external writers at no extra cost on any plan.

pros: Strong focus on content quality and semantic relevance rather than generic keyword stuffing
Native Google Docs and WordPress integrations fit existing writer workflows
Extended into AI search visibility tracking (ChatGPT, Gemini) ahead of many competitors
Used and trusted by large enterprise content teams (Adobe, HubSpot, Shopify)
cons: Starting price of $129/month is higher than several content-optimization competitors
Page and prompt limits on lower tiers can be restrictive for high-volume content teams
Enterprise pricing is not public and requires a sales conversation
Focused narrowly on content optimization rather than full-suite SEO (backlinks, technical audits)

faqs.question: What does Clearscope do?
faqs.answer: Clearscope analyzes top-ranking search results for a target keyword and gives writers a content grade, recommended terms, and related topics to help their content rank better in Google and be surfaced in AI answer engines.

faqs.question: How much does Clearscope cost?
faqs.answer: Clearscope starts at $129/month for the Essentials plan, with a Business plan at $399/month and custom Enterprise pricing for larger organizations.

faqs.question: Does Clearscope integrate with Google Docs and WordPress?
faqs.answer: Yes, Clearscope integrates directly with both Google Docs and WordPress so writers can see optimization scores and recommendations while writing.

faqs.question: Can Clearscope track AI search visibility, not just Google rankings?
faqs.answer: Yes. Clearscope tracks prompts across ChatGPT and Gemini to monitor how often and how a brand or page is referenced in AI-generated answers, in addition to traditional search optimization.

faqs.question: Who is Clearscope built for?
faqs.answer: Clearscope is built for content marketers, SEO teams, and agencies that produce ongoing organic-search content and need a structured, data-driven way to brief and grade writers.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Clearscope?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives in the content optimization space include Surfer SEO, MarketMuse, and Frase.

use_cases.title: SEO content briefs at scale
use_cases.description: Content teams generate data-backed briefs with target terms and topics for freelance or in-house writers before drafting begins.

use_cases.title: Content audits and refreshes
use_cases.description: Teams re-grade existing pages against current top-ranking competitors to identify gaps and refresh underperforming content.

use_cases.title: AI answer engine visibility tracking
use_cases.description: Brands track how often they're cited in ChatGPT and Gemini responses for key prompts and adjust content to improve visibility.

enrichment.short_description: Clerk is a developer-focused authentication and user management platform offering prebuilt sign-in flows, UI components, and enterprise SSO for modern web apps.
enrichment.seo_title: Clerk Review: Authentication & User Management Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Clerk provides prebuilt auth, user management, and SSO for developers. Compare pricing plans, MAU limits, features, pros, cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Clerk is a developer-focused authentication and user management platform founded in 2019 by twin brothers Braden Sidoti and Colin Sidoti, headquartered in San Francisco, California. It provides prebuilt, customizable UI components and backend infrastructure for sign-up, sign-in, user profile management, organizations/multi-tenancy, and session handling, aimed at letting engineering teams add production-grade authentication to a web or mobile app in hours rather than building it from scratch. Clerk supports modern frameworks including Next.js, React, Remix, and others, and exposes both drop-in UI components and lower-level APIs and SDKs for teams that want full control over the interface. Core authentication protocols, including SAML, OIDC, and enterprise SSO, are available across all pricing tiers rather than gated to the most expensive plan, which differentiates Clerk from some competitors that reserve enterprise identity protocols for top-tier contracts. Clerk's pricing is usage-based on Monthly Retained Users (MRU), a metric similar to MAU but that only counts users who return more than 24 hours after signup. The Hobby plan is free and includes up to 50,000 MRUs per app, unlimited applications, and up to three dashboard seats, making it usable for side projects and early-stage startups without a credit card. The Pro plan costs $25/month ($20/month billed annually) and includes 50,000 MRUs with additional users billed at $0.02 per MRU beyond that, plus branding removal and multi-factor authentication; optional add-ons like custom session lifetime and enterprise SSO connections cost $75/month each on Pro. The Business plan costs $300/month ($250/month annually), raising the dashboard seat count to ten, adding a SOC 2 report, priority support, and 30-day application log retention, with volume pricing on MRU overages. Enterprise plans are custom-priced and add a 99.99% uptime SLA, dedicated support, HIPAA compliance with a signed BAA, custom log retention, and SIEM integration. Clerk is best suited for startups and product teams building modern web applications that want to avoid maintaining custom auth infrastructure, particularly teams already using React-based frameworks. Its primary competitors include Auth0, Okta, Firebase Authentication, and Supabase Auth, with Clerk generally positioned as a more developer-experience-focused, UI-component-first alternative relative to older enterprise identity platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Clerk was founded in 2019 by twin brothers Braden Sidoti and Colin Sidoti in San Francisco to give application developers a faster path to production-ready authentication than building it themselves or integrating a heavier legacy identity platform. The product combines prebuilt, customizable UI components with backend user-management infrastructure, so teams can add sign-up, sign-in, profile management, and organizations to an app without owning the underlying security logic.

Clerk has grown to support most major web frameworks, with especially deep integration for React-based stacks like Next.js and Remix, and now serves startups through large engineering teams that need enterprise identity features such as SAML and SSO without switching to a dedicated identity provider.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Clerk ships drop-in UI components for sign-up, sign-in, user profile, and organization management that can be styled to match an application's design, along with lower-level SDKs and APIs for teams that want to build fully custom interfaces on top of Clerk's auth backend. It supports multi-factor authentication, social login providers, and passkeys alongside traditional email/password flows.

Enterprise identity protocols, SAML, OIDC, and SSO, are included on every paid tier rather than reserved for the top plan, and Clerk supports multi-tenant "organizations" out of the box for B2B SaaS applications that need to manage teams and roles within a single account structure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Clerk's Hobby plan is free and includes up to 50,000 Monthly Retained Users (MRUs) per application, unlimited applications, and up to three dashboard seats. The Pro plan is $25/month ($20/month billed annually), includes the same 50,000 MRU allotment with overages at $0.02 per additional MRU, and adds branding removal and MFA; enterprise SSO connections and custom session lifetime are available as $75/month add-ons.

The Business plan is $300/month ($250/month billed annually) and adds a SOC 2 report, priority support, 30-day log retention, and ten dashboard seats. Enterprise pricing is custom and adds a 99.99% uptime SLA, HIPAA/BAA support, custom log retention, and SIEM integration.

features.title: Prebuilt sign-up and sign-in UI components
features.description: Drop-in, customizable components for authentication flows that can be styled to match an application without building UI from scratch.

features.title: Multi-factor authentication
features.description: Built-in MFA options to add an additional verification step to sign-in flows.

features.title: Enterprise SSO, SAML, and OIDC
features.description: Enterprise identity protocols available on all paid tiers, not restricted to top-tier plans.

features.title: Organizations and multi-tenancy
features.description: Native support for teams, roles, and multi-tenant account structures for B2B SaaS applications.

features.title: Framework SDKs and APIs
features.description: SDKs for React, Next.js, Remix, and other frameworks, plus lower-level APIs for fully custom auth UIs.

features.title: Session management
features.description: Configurable session lifetimes and secure session handling across web and mobile clients.

features.title: Compliance and audit features
features.description: SOC 2 reporting, HIPAA/BAA support, and SIEM integration available on Business and Enterprise plans.

pros: Free Hobby tier supports up to 50,000 monthly retained users with no credit card
Enterprise SSO/SAML/OIDC included on all paid tiers, not gated to Enterprise only
Strong developer experience with prebuilt UI components and deep React/Next.js support
Native multi-tenant organizations feature simplifies B2B SaaS account structures
cons: MRU-based pricing can become expensive at scale compared to flat-rate competitors
Dashboard seat limits on lower tiers may require upgrading for larger teams
Some enterprise-grade add-ons (custom session lifetime, SSO connections) cost extra even on Pro
Business and Enterprise plans represent a large price jump from Pro ($25 to $300+/month)

faqs.question: Is Clerk free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Clerk's Hobby plan is free and includes up to 50,000 Monthly Retained Users (MRUs) per application, unlimited applications, and up to three dashboard seats.

faqs.question: What is a Monthly Retained User (MRU) in Clerk's pricing?
faqs.answer: An MRU is a user who returns to the application more than 24 hours after signing up, similar to but stricter than a typical Monthly Active User (MAU) metric used by other platforms.

faqs.question: Does Clerk support enterprise SSO and SAML?
faqs.answer: Yes, and unlike many competitors, Clerk includes SAML, OIDC, and enterprise SSO on all of its paid tiers rather than only its most expensive Enterprise plan.

faqs.question: What frameworks does Clerk support?
faqs.answer: Clerk provides SDKs for React, Next.js, Remix, and other modern web frameworks, along with APIs for custom integrations.

faqs.question: How much does Clerk cost at scale?
faqs.answer: Beyond the included 50,000 MRUs on the Pro plan, additional users are billed at $0.02 per MRU. The Business plan costs $300/month ($250/month annually) with volume pricing on overages, and Enterprise is custom-priced.

faqs.question: Who is Clerk best suited for?
faqs.answer: Clerk is best suited for startups and product teams building modern web applications, particularly React-based apps, that want production-ready authentication without building it in-house.

use_cases.title: Adding authentication to a new web app
use_cases.description: Startups drop in Clerk's prebuilt sign-up and sign-in components to launch with production-grade auth in hours instead of building it from scratch.

use_cases.title: B2B SaaS multi-tenant account management
use_cases.description: Product teams use Clerk's Organizations feature to manage teams, roles, and permissions within a multi-tenant B2B application.

use_cases.title: Enterprise SSO for regulated applications
use_cases.description: Companies serving enterprise customers use Clerk's SAML/SSO support to meet customer identity requirements without switching identity providers.

features: Enterprise SSO connections available as $75/mo add-on

enrichment.short_description: Clever Cloud is a French Platform-as-a-Service that auto-deploys and auto-scales applications and managed databases with git push, billed by the second.
enrichment.seo_title: Clever Cloud Review: European PaaS Hosting & Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Clever Cloud is a European PaaS for auto-scaling app hosting and managed databases. See pricing, features, supported languages, pros, cons, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Clever Cloud is a Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) provider founded in 2010 in Nantes, France, built around European, GDPR-compliant infrastructure with data centers located in France, including Paris and the Roubaix region. The platform lets developers deploy applications with a simple git push and have the platform automatically handle scaling, monitoring, and maintenance, without managing servers or containers directly. It supports more than 25 programming languages and frameworks, and offers fully managed databases alongside compute so teams can run both application and data layers on the same platform. Clever Cloud's defining pricing characteristic is per-second, usage-based billing rather than fixed monthly plan tiers: customers fund their account with an amount reflecting expected usage, resources auto-scale within a maximum price ceiling the customer sets, and the bill adjusts monthly to match actual consumption. New users can sign up and receive free credits to test the platform without entering a payment card. There is no published flat "starting price" because cost depends entirely on the compute, storage, and database resources selected; the company states that customers migrating from other providers see cost reductions of roughly 30% on average due to more precise resource allocation and the absence of over-provisioning. Clever Cloud does not offer a permanent free tier for production use, and per-usage billing means costs can vary during traffic spikes unless a maximum spending cap is configured. The company is a bootstrapped, profitable business reporting roughly $6.2 million in annual recurring revenue and employs an estimated 70-100+ people, positioned in the 51-200 employee range. It is best suited for European companies and developers who prioritize data sovereignty and want automatic, git-based deployment and scaling without managing Kubernetes or raw cloud infrastructure themselves, and it is commonly chosen as a European alternative to platforms like Heroku, Render, or AWS Elastic Beanstalk for teams that need EU-only data residency. Enterprise and startup programs with custom terms are available directly from Clever Cloud's sales team for organizations with larger or more specialized infrastructure needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Clever Cloud was founded in 2010 in Nantes, France, with a focus on building a European alternative to US-based PaaS and cloud providers. The company operates its infrastructure entirely within EU borders, with data centers in France, and positions GDPR compliance and data sovereignty as core parts of its value proposition rather than an add-on.

The platform's core workflow is deploy-by-git-push: developers push code and Clever Cloud handles builds, scaling, monitoring, and infrastructure maintenance automatically, supporting more than 25 languages and frameworks alongside fully managed database services.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Applications deploy via a standard git push, with the platform building and running the app without requiring developers to write Dockerfiles or manage container orchestration directly. Compute resources auto-scale based on load, and customers can set a maximum price they're willing to incur so scaling doesn't produce unpredictable bills.

Clever Cloud offers fully managed databases (relational and other engines) alongside compute, so a team can run its entire application and data layer on the same platform. All infrastructure runs on European data centers, supporting organizations that need to keep data processing within the EU for regulatory or contractual reasons.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Clever Cloud bills by the second based on actual resource consumption rather than offering fixed monthly plan tiers. Customers fund their account with a balance that reflects expected usage, and that amount is adjusted month to month based on real consumption, with an interactive calculator on the pricing page for estimating costs by product and region.

New accounts receive free credits to test the platform without a payment card. There is no permanent free production tier, and because billing follows usage, traffic spikes can increase costs unless a spending ceiling is configured; startup and enterprise programs with custom pricing are available by contacting sales.

features.title: Git-push deployment
features.description: Deploy applications by pushing to a git repository; the platform builds and runs the app automatically.

features.title: Automatic scaling
features.description: Compute resources scale up or down based on load, with a customer-set maximum price ceiling to control costs.

features.title: Fully managed databases
features.description: Managed database services run alongside application compute on the same platform.

features.title: Broad language and framework support
features.description: Supports more than 25 programming languages and frameworks out of the box.

features.title: European, GDPR-compliant infrastructure
features.description: All data processing occurs on infrastructure located in France, supporting EU data residency requirements.

features.title: Per-second usage billing
features.description: Costs are metered by the second based on actual resource consumption rather than flat monthly tiers.

features.title: Free trial credits
features.description: New signups receive complimentary credits to test the platform without providing payment details.

pros: European, GDPR-compliant infrastructure with data centers in France
Simple git-push deployment with automatic scaling and maintenance
Fully managed databases alongside compute on one platform
Per-second usage billing can reduce costs versus over-provisioned fixed plans
Free trial credits available without requiring a payment card
cons: No permanent free tier for production workloads
Usage-based billing can produce unpredictable bills during traffic spikes without a spending cap set
No flat published starting price, since cost depends on selected resources
Smaller ecosystem and community compared to larger US-based PaaS competitors

faqs.question: What is Clever Cloud?
faqs.answer: Clever Cloud is a French Platform-as-a-Service that lets developers deploy applications with a git push while the platform automatically handles scaling, monitoring, and infrastructure maintenance, running entirely on European data centers.

faqs.question: How does Clever Cloud pricing work?
faqs.answer: Clever Cloud bills by the second based on actual resource usage rather than fixed monthly plans. Customers fund an account balance reflecting expected usage and can set a maximum spending ceiling to control costs during scaling.

faqs.question: Does Clever Cloud have a free tier?
faqs.answer: There is no permanent free production tier, but new accounts receive free trial credits to test the platform without entering a payment card.

faqs.question: Is Clever Cloud GDPR compliant?
faqs.answer: Yes. Clever Cloud operates exclusively on infrastructure located in France and the EU, and the company states it is fully GDPR compliant.

faqs.question: What languages and frameworks does Clever Cloud support?
faqs.answer: Clever Cloud supports more than 25 programming languages and frameworks, along with fully managed database services alongside application compute.

faqs.question: Who should use Clever Cloud instead of AWS or Heroku?
faqs.answer: Clever Cloud is a strong fit for teams that need EU-only data residency and prefer simple, automatic deployment and scaling over managing raw cloud infrastructure or Kubernetes themselves.

use_cases.title: EU data-sovereign application hosting
use_cases.description: Companies that must keep application and database infrastructure within EU borders for regulatory reasons deploy on Clever Cloud's French data centers.
use_cases.audience: European companies with GDPR or data residency requirements

use_cases.title: Simplified deployment for small engineering teams
use_cases.description: Teams without dedicated DevOps resources use git-push deployment and automatic scaling to avoid managing servers or Kubernetes directly.

use_cases.title: Managed application plus database hosting
use_cases.description: Startups run both their application compute and managed databases on a single platform instead of stitching together separate hosting and database vendors.

enrichment.short_description: ClickFunnels is an all-in-one sales funnel and website builder that lets entrepreneurs create landing pages, courses, and marketing automations without coding.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ClickFunnels is an all-in-one funnel and website builder for entrepreneurs. Compare pricing plans, features, pros, cons, and who it's best suited for.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ClickFunnels is a no-code sales funnel and website-building platform founded in October 2014 by Russell Brunson and Todd Dickerson under their entity Etison LLC, headquartered in Eagle, Idaho. It lets entrepreneurs, coaches, and small businesses build landing pages, checkout flows, membership sites, and online courses, and chain them into automated marketing "funnels" without writing code. The company grew without venture capital funding, reaching over $100 million in annual sales and more than 100,000 paying customers by 2019, and surpassing $265 million in annual revenue with roughly 150,000 customers by 2023. ClickFunnels 2.0, the current version of the platform, bundles page building, email and SMS marketing automation, course/membership hosting, an affiliate program manager, and a built-in CRM into a single subscription rather than requiring separate tools. Pricing is tiered by workspace, contact, and email-send limits: the Launch plan costs $97/month and includes one workspace, two team members, 10,000 contacts, 50,000 emails per month, three courses, and five custom domains; the Scale plan (the most popular tier) costs $197/month with five workspaces, 75,000 contacts, 300,000 emails per month, and custom code editor and API access; the Optimize plan costs $297/month with ten workspaces and 150,000 contacts; and an annual-only Dominate plan costs $5,997/year with 20 workspaces, 400,000 contacts, and VIP support with private onboarding. All monthly plans include unlimited funnels and visitors and a 14-day free trial, and higher tiers include increasing allotments of built-in AI-generated words for copywriting assistance. ClickFunnels is best suited for solo entrepreneurs, coaches, course creators, and small marketing teams that want to launch and iterate on sales funnels quickly without hiring developers or stitching together separate landing-page, email, and checkout tools. It is less suited to businesses that need deep custom development, enterprise-grade e-commerce catalog management, or highly technical, code-first website control. Its primary competitors include Kartra, Kajabi, GoHighLevel, and more general website builders like Leadpages, with ClickFunnels differentiated by its strong emphasis on Russell Brunson's funnel-marketing methodology and its large library of published training content and templates.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ClickFunnels launched in October 2014, built by Russell Brunson and Todd Dickerson to let marketers and entrepreneurs assemble complete sales funnels, landing pages, order forms, upsells, and follow-up sequences, without needing a developer or a separate stack of tools. The company grew primarily through Brunson's own marketing audience and bootstrapped its growth without outside venture funding, becoming one of the fastest-growing privately held SaaS companies in North America by the late 2010s.

The current version of the platform, ClickFunnels 2.0, consolidates page building, email/SMS automation, course and membership hosting, affiliate management, and CRM functionality into one product, aiming to replace the need for a marketer to combine a page builder, an email tool, a course platform, and a CRM separately.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Users build funnels visually with a drag-and-drop editor, choosing from templates for opt-in pages, sales pages, checkout flows, and upsell sequences, then connect them into a complete customer journey. Built-in email and SMS marketing automation lets teams follow up with leads and customers without a separate email service provider.

The platform also includes course and membership site hosting for creators selling digital products, an affiliate program manager for running a referral partner program, and, on higher tiers, a custom code editor and API access for teams that want to extend the platform beyond its no-code building blocks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ClickFunnels offers three monthly plans: Launch at $97/month (1 workspace, 10K contacts, 3 courses), Scale at $197/month (5 workspaces, 75K contacts, API access), and Optimize at $297/month (10 workspaces, 150K contacts). An annual-only Dominate plan costs $5,997/year with 20 workspaces, 400K contacts, and VIP support with private onboarding.

All monthly plans include unlimited funnels and unlimited visitors, and every plan comes with a 14-day free trial. Higher tiers include larger allotments of built-in AI-generated words for on-platform copywriting assistance, along with priority or live-chat support.

features.description: Run a referral or affiliate partner program with tracking and payouts built into ClickFunnels.

features.title: AI copywriting assistance
features.description: Generate marketing copy using an included AI word allotment that scales with plan tier.

features.title: Custom code editor and API access
features.description: Available on Scale and higher tiers for teams that want to extend or customize beyond the no-code builder.

features.title: Funnel and page templates
features.description: A library of pre-built funnel and page templates based on Russell Brunson's funnel-marketing frameworks.

No-code funnel builder is accessible to non-technical entrepreneurs
Large library of templates and training content built around a proven funnel methodology
Unlimited funnels and visitors included on every plan
14-day free trial available on all monthly plans
cons: Starting price of $97/month is higher than many standalone landing-page builders
API access and custom code editing are limited to Scale and higher tiers
Contact and email-send limits can require an upgrade as a list grows
Less suited to complex e-commerce catalogs than dedicated e-commerce platforms

faqs.question: What is ClickFunnels used for?
faqs.answer: ClickFunnels is used to build sales funnels, combining landing pages, checkout flows, upsells, email/SMS follow-up, and course or membership delivery, without needing to code or assemble separate tools.

faqs.question: How much does ClickFunnels cost?
faqs.answer: ClickFunnels starts at $97/month for the Launch plan, with Scale at $197/month and Optimize at $297/month. An annual-only Dominate plan costs $5,997/year. All monthly plans include a 14-day free trial.

faqs.question: Who founded ClickFunnels?
faqs.answer: ClickFunnels was founded in October 2014 by Russell Brunson and Todd Dickerson, launched without venture capital funding under their entity Etison LLC.

faqs.question: Does ClickFunnels include email marketing?
faqs.answer: Yes, built-in email and SMS marketing automation is included on all plans, with sending limits that increase at higher tiers.

faqs.question: Can I sell online courses on ClickFunnels?
faqs.answer: Yes, ClickFunnels includes course and membership site hosting, with the number of courses allowed increasing by plan tier.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to ClickFunnels?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Kartra, Kajabi, GoHighLevel, and landing-page-focused tools like Leadpages, depending on whether a business needs full funnel/CRM functionality or just page building.

Custom code editor, API access, 5M AI words

enrichment.short_description: ClickHelp is a cloud-based technical documentation platform for creating, hosting, translating, and publishing user manuals, API docs, and knowledge bases.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ClickHelp is a cloud documentation platform for technical writers. Compare pricing plans, single-sourcing features, translation tools, pros, and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ClickHelp is a cloud-based online documentation platform founded in 2010 in Yerevan, Armenia, built for technical writers and product teams that need to create, host, and publish user manuals, API documentation, and knowledge bases without installing desktop authoring software. The platform is entirely browser-based, letting distributed writing teams collaborate on the same documentation projects in real time. Its core capabilities center on single-source publishing, writing content once and reusing it across multiple outputs (web help, PDF, CHM, and other formats) and multiple product versions, which reduces duplicate maintenance work when documenting several releases of the same product in parallel. ClickHelp also supports API documentation, translation and localization management for multilingual documentation, in-depth analytics on how readers use published docs, and content migration tools that import existing documentation from Word, HTML, CHM, and OpenOffice formats. Enterprise features include role-based permissions, context-sensitive help configuration for embedding docs inside a software product's UI, custom branding with CSS and template customization, single sign-on, and SOC 2 Type II certification with GDPR and CCPA compliance. Pricing is tiered by monthly subscription: Starter costs $185/month, Growth costs $310/month, and Professional costs $610/month, with higher tiers unlocking more authors, storage, and advanced features like translation workflows and analytics depth. ClickHelp reports roughly 25,000 customers across more than 100 countries, including enterprise clients like IBM, and generates an estimated $2.9 million in annual revenue with a small team of around 17 employees, making it a compact but established player in the technical documentation software category. It is best suited for software companies and technical writing teams that maintain documentation across multiple product versions or need to publish the same content across several output formats and languages, rather than teams that need only a simple, single-format help file. Its main competitors include MadCap Flare, Document360, Confluence (for internal knowledge bases), and Paligo, with ClickHelp generally positioned as a fully cloud-hosted alternative to older desktop-based authoring tools like MadCap Flare or RoboHelp.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ClickHelp was founded in 2010 in Yerevan, Armenia, to give technical writing teams a fully cloud-based alternative to desktop documentation authoring tools, letting distributed teams write, review, and publish documentation collaboratively from a browser rather than syncing files between local installations.

The platform is used by software vendors and product teams to produce user manuals, API references, and knowledge bases, with a particular emphasis on single-sourcing: writing content once and reusing it across multiple product versions and output formats rather than maintaining separate copies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Single-source publishing lets teams maintain one set of content and generate multiple outputs, web help, PDF, and CHM, from it, while multi-version documentation management keeps docs for several product releases organized in parallel. Built-in translation management supports localization workflows for teams publishing documentation in multiple languages.

ClickHelp also includes API documentation support, in-depth analytics on reader behavior within published docs, context-sensitive help configuration for embedding documentation inside a software product, and content migration tools that import existing docs from Word, HTML, CHM, and OpenOffice.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ClickHelp is sold as a monthly subscription with three tiers: Starter at $185/month, Growth at $310/month, and Professional at $610/month (all prices exclude tax). Higher tiers increase author seats, storage, and access to advanced features like deeper analytics and translation workflows.

The platform includes enterprise-grade security as standard, SOC 2 Type II certification, GDPR/CCPA compliance, AES-256 encryption, and a 99.9% uptime SLA with daily automatic backups, across its paid tiers.

features.description: Import existing documentation from Word, HTML, CHM, and OpenOffice into ClickHelp.

Strong single-sourcing and multi-version documentation support
Enterprise security standard across tiers (SOC 2 Type II, GDPR/CCPA, AES-256)
Built-in translation and localization management for multilingual docs
cons: Starting price of $185/month is higher than several documentation tool competitors
Smaller company and team size relative to larger documentation platform vendors
Full feature set (translation workflows, deep analytics) concentrated in higher tiers
Less brand recognition than desktop-authoring incumbents like MadCap Flare

faqs.question: What is ClickHelp used for?
faqs.answer: ClickHelp is used to create, host, and publish technical documentation, including user manuals, API references, and knowledge bases, entirely from a browser-based platform.

faqs.question: How much does ClickHelp cost?
faqs.answer: ClickHelp's plans start at $185/month for Starter, $310/month for Growth, and $610/month for Professional, excluding tax.

faqs.question: Does ClickHelp support multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, ClickHelp includes translation and localization management features for teams publishing documentation in multiple languages.

faqs.question: What is single-sourcing in ClickHelp?
faqs.answer: Single-sourcing means writing content once and reusing it across multiple output formats (web help, PDF, CHM) and multiple product versions, instead of maintaining separate copies of the same content.

faqs.question: Can I migrate existing documentation into ClickHelp?
faqs.answer: Yes, ClickHelp provides content migration tools that import documentation from Word, HTML, CHM, and OpenOffice formats.

faqs.question: Who uses ClickHelp?
faqs.answer: ClickHelp is used by software vendors and technical writing teams, including enterprise clients like IBM, to manage documentation across multiple product versions and languages.

enrichment.short_description: ClickHouse is an open-source, column-oriented database built for real-time analytics at scale, available self-hosted for free or as the managed ClickHouse Cloud.
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco Bay Area, United States (with ClickHouse B.V. in Amsterdam, Netherlands)
enrichment.seo_title: ClickHouse Review: Open-Source Analytics Database & Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ClickHouse is an open-source columnar database for real-time analytics. Compare self-hosted vs ClickHouse Cloud pricing, features, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ClickHouse is an open-source, column-oriented database management system built for online analytical processing (OLAP), designed to run SQL queries over very large datasets and return results in real time. The underlying technology originated in 2009 when Alexey Milovidov and collaborators began developing it to power Yandex.Metrica, one of the world's largest web analytics platforms, and it was released as open source in 2016. ClickHouse Inc. was incorporated in San Francisco in September 2021 to commercialize the database, with a subsidiary, ClickHouse B.V., based in Amsterdam, Netherlands, giving the company dual US and European operating bases. Since its open-source release, ClickHouse has been adopted by companies including Uber, Comcast, eBay, and Cisco for high-volume analytics workloads such as observability, product analytics, and real-time reporting. The core database remains free and open source and can be self-hosted on any infrastructure, with no licensing cost, though operating it at scale requires managing the underlying servers, storage, and cluster configuration directly. For teams that prefer a managed offering, ClickHouse Inc. sells ClickHouse Cloud, a serverless, fully managed version of the database billed separately for compute and storage: compute is metered per minute in increments tied to available memory, with rates that increase by tier (roughly $0.22/unit-hour on the Basic tier, $0.30 on Scale, and $0.39 on Enterprise), while storage is billed at a flat rate of roughly $25 per terabyte-month across all tiers. ClickHouse Cloud has three named tiers: Basic, aimed at experimentation and small-scale analytics rather than production high-availability workloads, with realistic monthly costs often in the $66-190 range depending on usage; Scale, starting around $499/month, built for production workloads needing replica and shard scaling; and Enterprise, which adds compliance features like HIPAA/PCI support, SAML/SSO, and customer-managed encryption keys, with typical bills ranging roughly from $2,700 to $9,700/month depending on configuration. New ClickHouse Cloud accounts get a 30-day trial with $300 in credit. The company has raised significant venture funding and reached a valuation in the billions of dollars, with annualized revenue reported at roughly $100 million as ClickHouse Cloud has scaled since its 2022 launch. ClickHouse is best suited for engineering teams building analytics, observability, or reporting systems that need to query billions of rows with low latency, and is generally not the right choice for transactional (OLTP) workloads like typical application databases, where row-oriented systems like PostgreSQL are a better fit.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ClickHouse's technology traces back to 2009, when Alexey Milovidov and collaborators began building it to power Yandex.Metrica's web analytics at massive scale; it first went into production in 2012 and was released as open source in 2016. ClickHouse Inc. was incorporated in San Francisco in September 2021 to commercialize the open-source database, with a European subsidiary, ClickHouse B.V., based in Amsterdam.

The database is a column-oriented OLAP system, optimized for running analytical SQL queries over very large datasets with low latency, and has been adopted by companies including Uber, Comcast, eBay, and Cisco for observability, product analytics, and real-time reporting workloads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ClickHouse's columnar storage engine is built specifically for read-heavy analytical queries over billions of rows, supporting standard SQL along with extensions for time-series and analytical functions. It supports high compression ratios, real-time data ingestion, and distributed, sharded deployments for horizontal scale.

The self-hosted, open-source edition can be deployed on any infrastructure with no licensing cost, while ClickHouse Cloud, the company's managed offering, adds serverless auto-scaling, automated backups, and built-in high availability without requiring a team to operate the underlying cluster themselves.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core ClickHouse database is free and open source for self-hosted use, with no licensing fees regardless of scale. ClickHouse Cloud, the managed serverless offering, separates billing into compute (metered per minute, roughly $0.22-$0.39 per compute unit-hour depending on tier) and storage (a flat rate of about $25 per terabyte-month across all tiers).

ClickHouse Cloud has three tiers: Basic for experimentation and small workloads, Scale starting around $499/month for production workloads needing replica and shard scaling, and Enterprise for organizations needing compliance features like HIPAA/PCI support, SAML/SSO, and customer-managed encryption keys. New accounts get a 30-day trial with $300 in credit before choosing a paid plan or self-hosting.

features.title: Column-oriented storage engine
features.description: Optimized for analytical queries over large datasets, with high compression and fast aggregate scans.

features.title: Real-time query performance
features.description: Designed to return results over billions of rows with low latency for dashboards and reporting.

features.title: Distributed, sharded architecture
features.description: Scales horizontally across nodes for very large datasets and high query concurrency.

features.title: Standard SQL support
features.description: Query with familiar SQL syntax plus extensions for time-series and analytical functions.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Run the open-source database on any infrastructure with no licensing cost.

features.title: ClickHouse Cloud (serverless managed service)
features.description: Fully managed, auto-scaling version of the database with separate compute and storage billing.

features.title: High availability and automated backups
features.description: Available on ClickHouse Cloud without requiring manual cluster operations.

features.title: Enterprise compliance features
features.description: HIPAA/PCI support, SAML/SSO, and customer-managed encryption keys on the Enterprise Cloud tier.

pros: Core database is free, open source, and can be self-hosted with no licensing cost
Very strong performance for analytical queries over large datasets
Proven at scale by companies including Uber, Comcast, and Cisco
ClickHouse Cloud removes operational burden of running clusters manually
30-day trial with $300 in credit for evaluating ClickHouse Cloud
cons: Not designed for transactional (OLTP) workloads; row-oriented databases are a better fit for those
Self-hosting at scale requires real operational expertise to manage clusters and storage
ClickHouse Cloud costs can rise quickly for production workloads on the Scale and Enterprise tiers
Separate compute and storage billing on Cloud can make cost estimation less straightforward than flat pricing

faqs.question: Is ClickHouse free?
faqs.answer: The core ClickHouse database is free and open source and can be self-hosted on any infrastructure with no licensing cost. ClickHouse Cloud, the managed serverless offering, is billed separately for compute and storage.

faqs.question: What is ClickHouse used for?
faqs.answer: ClickHouse is used for real-time analytics, observability, and reporting workloads that require running SQL queries over very large datasets with low latency, such as web analytics, log analysis, and business intelligence dashboards.

faqs.question: How much does ClickHouse Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: ClickHouse Cloud pricing separates compute (roughly $0.22-$0.39 per compute unit-hour depending on tier) and storage (about $25/TB-month flat). The Basic tier can run in the range of $66-190/month depending on usage, the Scale tier starts around $499/month, and Enterprise pricing depends on configuration.

faqs.question: Who founded ClickHouse and when?
faqs.answer: The technology originated in 2009 when Alexey Milovidov and collaborators began building it to power Yandex.Metrica; it was open-sourced in 2016, and ClickHouse Inc. was incorporated in San Francisco in September 2021 to commercialize it.

faqs.question: Is ClickHouse good for transactional (OLTP) applications?
faqs.answer: No. ClickHouse is optimized for analytical (OLAP) workloads over large datasets, not for typical transactional application databases; row-oriented databases like PostgreSQL are generally a better fit for OLTP use cases.

faqs.question: What companies use ClickHouse?
faqs.answer: Companies including Uber, Comcast, eBay, and Cisco use ClickHouse for high-volume analytics, observability, and reporting workloads.

use_cases.title: Real-time observability and log analytics
use_cases.description: Engineering teams query billions of log and metric events with low latency to power observability dashboards.

use_cases.title: Product and web analytics at scale

use_cases.title: Customer-facing analytics dashboards
